perturbation.original_text: You hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the United States of America located in the State of New York for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of or relating to this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby (and you agree not to commence any action, suit or proceeding relating thereto except in such courts), and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to your address set forth above shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against you in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: You hereby consent to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of New York for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of or relating to this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby. You agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by mail to your address set forth above shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against you in any such court.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the words 'irrevocably', 'unconditionally', and 'exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the United States of America' weakens the jurisdiction clause. This could allow a party to argue that they can bring the suit in a different jurisdiction outside of the United States or that they can revoke consent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit mention of the 'exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the United States of America' and weakening the consent to jurisdiction, this modified text could allow a party to circumvent federal court jurisdiction based on diversity of citizenship as outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1332, particularly if the opposing party can demonstrate significant connections to another jurisdiction.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 'exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the United States of America', and consent is given 'irrevocably and unconditionally'. The modified text removes these qualifiers. The Law Explanation states that this weakening 'could allow a party to circumvent federal court jurisdiction'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 28 U.S.C. § 1332, concerns 'Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs', and it states 'The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions...and is between-(1) citizens of different States'. By removing the 'exclusive jurisdiction' clause, the modified text could contradict the original agreement to resolve disputes exclusively in U.S. courts, potentially conflicting with the jurisdiction established by 28 U.S.C. § 1332 if diversity of citizenship exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'entire' suggests that other agreements, whether written or oral, could also be considered part of the agreement between the parties. This weakens the agreement and can introduce ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 Statute of frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing 'entire', the clause weakens the enforceability of the agreement under New York's Statute of Frauds (NY GOL § 5-701), which requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed to be enforceable. If the agreement is not explicitly stated as the 'entire' agreement, parties could argue that other agreements (oral or written) exist, potentially invalidating the written agreement's exclusivity and enforceability.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'entire' from the original text significantly weakens the enforceability of the agreement under the Statute of Frauds, as described in the Law Explanation. The original clause explicitly states that the written agreement constitutes the 'entire agreement,' preventing parties from introducing extrinsic evidence of other agreements. The changed text, by omitting 'entire,' opens the door for parties to argue that other agreements (oral or written) exist, thereby undermining the exclusivity and enforceability of the written agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NY GOL § 5-701, emphasizes the requirement for certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By removing 'entire,' the modified text potentially allows for arguments that the written agreement does not fully represent the parties' understanding, thereby contradicting the purpose of the Statute of Frauds, which is to ensure that key agreements are documented in writing to avoid ambiguity and disputes.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that the Company would be irreparably injured and that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that the Company would be injured and that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to seek specific performance as remedies for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'irreparably,' softening 'money damages would not be a sufficient remedy' and omitting 'injunctive relief' significantly limits the remedies available to the Company. Deleting 'Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity' restricts them even further. This introduces uncertainty and legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit right to seek 'injunctive relief' and weakening the statement about irreparable harm and insufficient monetary damages, the modified text contradicts the principles underlying the availability of preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders under New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 6301. This law allows for injunctive relief when a party demonstrates irreparable injury and a likelihood of success on the merits. The modified contract language makes it harder for the Company to meet this standard.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-09-26 2014-10-03 2015-01-16 2015-04-24 2015-07-10 2015-09-04 2015-10-09 2015-10-30 2015-11-27 2015-12-18 2015-12-25 2016-01-22 2016-07-01 2016-07-29 2016-08-05 2016-08-26 2016-12-02 2016-12-23 2017-04-28 2017-06-02 2017-08-04 2017-08-25 2017-10-27 2018-02-02 2018-04-20 2018-07-13 2018-08-03 2018-08-31 2019-01-04 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-02-15 2019-03-01 2019-04-19 2019-06-28 2019-07-12 2019-08-16 2019-08-30 2019-09-06 2019-09-20 2019-10-11 2019-10-18 2019-10-25 2019-11-01 2019-11-08 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2019-12-20 2019-12-27 2020-04-10 2020-04-17 2020-04-24 2020-06-19 2020-10-16 2020-11-13 2021-02-05 2021-04-02 2021-04-23 2021-05-14 2021-06-18 2021-07-30 2021-08-06 2021-08-13 2021-10-15 2021-11-12 2021-12-03 2021-12-10 2021-12-31 2022-01-07 2022-01-28 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-04-08 2022-05-06 2022-05-13 2022-05-27 2022-06-03 2022-06-10 2022-06-17 2022-07-08 2022-09-02 2022-10-07 2022-12-02 2022-12-16 2022-12-23 2023-01-06 2023-05-12 2023-06-30 2023-07-07 2023-09-22 2023-10-27 2023-11-03 2023-11-17 2023-12-01 2023-12-15 2024-01-05 2024-02-02 2024-02-16 2024-03-01 2024-03-08 2024-05-03 2024-05-17 2024-07-05 2024-09-06 2024-10-11 2024-11-29 2024-12-20 2024-12-27 2025-02-28 2025-03-14 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 6301 Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 63 § 6301. Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the explicit right to seek 'injunctive relief' and weakens the statement about irreparable harm. This contradicts the principles underlying the availability of preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders under New York Civil Practice Law & Rules § 6301. The original text ensured the company could seek injunctive relief, while the modified text removes this explicit right, hindering the company's ability to obtain such relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 6301, states that a preliminary injunction may be granted to prevent acts violating the plaintiff's rights or causing injury during the action's pendency. The omission directly weakens the company's ability to invoke this law effectively, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof,' the modified text introduces ambiguity and the potential for a different jurisdiction's laws to apply based on conflict of laws principles. This can create uncertainty about the agreement's interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause ensures New York law governs, regardless of conflicts of law principles. Removing this subjects the agreement to New York's choice of law rules, as well as NY CLS Gen Oblig § 5-1401. This law dictates when parties can choose New York law to govern their contracts, but without the original clause's explicit exclusion of conflicts of law principles, a court might apply another jurisdiction's law if the transaction has significant connections to that jurisdiction, frustrating the parties' initial intent to be governed solely by New York law.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof' introduces a significant legal contradiction. The original clause ensures that New York law governs, irrespective of conflict of law principles, ensuring certainty. The changed text subjects the agreement to New York's choice of law rules which may result in the application of another jurisdiction's law. NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 allows parties to agree that New York law shall govern under certain conditions. Without the original clause excluding conflict of law principles, a court might apply another jurisdiction's law if the transaction has significant connections to that jurisdiction. This frustrates the intent to be governed solely by New York law.
